overview: This Mono-Black Midrange deck leverages early-game hand disruption to stabilize into a mid-game of resilient threats and graveyard recursion. It aims to outvalue opponents by consistently trading up with removal and hand size advantage, eventually deploying a dominant late-game finisher. The strategy relies on controlling the board state while accumulating resources for a decisive endgame.
win_conditions: Deploying Sheoldred, the Apocalypse to drain opponent life and remove threats
Attacking with a board of resilient creatures like Graveyard Trespasser
Outlasting opponents through consistent hand advantage and resource denial

keep_criteria: Hands with Thoughtseize and a one or two-drop threat
Hands with multiple disruption spells to control the early game
Hands that enable a turn-one or turn-two play
Keep hands with 2-3 lands, a Thoughtseize, and a turn-2 threat or removal.
Prioritize hands that can play a land, Thoughtseize, and a 2-drop by turn 2.
mulligan_criteria: Hands lacking early disruption or threats
Hands with only high-cost cards like Sheoldred
Hands with no lands or unplayable mana bases
Mulligan if you have 0-1 lands, multiple Thoughtseizes with no threats, or only high-cost spells without answers.

early_game: Cast Thoughtseize on turn one to disrupt opponent's game plan
Play low-cost threats to establish board presence
Use disruption to protect key cards or set up combos
Turn 1: Play a land, Thoughtseize if possible (target opponent's key combo piece, early threat, or removal).
Turn 2: Play a land, cast a 2-drop threat or removal.
If opponent plays a creature, answer it
if they play a land, hold Thoughtseize for later or use it on a key spell.
Land sequencing: Prioritize basic swamps or duals that enter untapped if possible to ensure mana consistency for Thoughtseize and early plays.
mid_game: Deploy Graveyard Trespasser to apply pressure and enable graveyard synergies
Trade efficiently with removal and hand size advantage
Begin accumulating resources for late-game finishers
Preserve Thoughtseize for critical turns (opponent's draw step or when they have a key combo piece).
Don't overcommit mana to threats if you lack answers.
Use the graveyard strategically: feed it to Trespasser for value, but avoid giving Sheoldred or other recursion targets to opponent graveyard hate unless necessary.
Manage hand size: keep 3-4 cards minimum to weather disruption.
Commit threats when you have removal ready for their answers.
Hold back if you suspect a sweep or if you need to preserve mana for disruption.
late_game: Cast Sheoldred, the Apocalypse to control the board and drain life
Leverage graveyard recursion to outvalue opponents
Close out games with overwhelming board presence and life total advantage
Sheoldred, the Apocalypse is the primary win condition.
Get it on board and protected with removal or discard.
Graveyard Trespasser can close games by cycling through their graveyard and dealing damage or drawing cards.
Go all-in when you have Sheoldred on board, opponent has no sweepers, and you have hand disruption to protect your threats.
Also commit when you can lock them out of their combo or control game plan.

key_cards.name: Thoughtseize
key_cards.notes: Use on turn one to remove key threats or combo pieces, or hold to protect your own threats

key_cards.name: Sheoldred, the Apocalypse
key_cards.role: Late-game win condition and value engine
key_cards.notes: Deploy when you have board control to drain life and remove opponent's threats, leveraging its death triggers

key_cards.name: Graveyard Trespasser
key_cards.notes: Play to apply pressure and enable recursion synergies, often serving as a resilient blocker or attacker

sequencing_tips: Hold Thoughtseize if opponent has a known turn-one play that disrupts your game plan
Prioritize playing threats before casting Sheoldred to maximize its death triggers
Use Graveyard Trespasser to enable graveyard recursion strategies before committing to late-game finishers
Common mistake: Overusing Thoughtseize on irrelevant cards.
Always target their most impactful spell or permanent.
Another mistake: Committing to a board without answers, leaving you vulnerable to sweepers.
Unique interaction: Graveyard Trespasser can be used to cycle your own graveyard to find value, but be careful not to feed Sheoldred or other key threats to opponent graveyard hate.
Also, track opponent's discard spells to know when to hold up hand.

matchups.advice: Use Thoughtseize to remove key early threats and stabilize with resilient blockers
Apply early pressure with threats and use hand advantage to outlast their removal
Disrupt their combo pieces with Thoughtseize and stabilize with mid-game threats

Use Thoughtseize to remove counterspells. Cast threats in response to their removal or when they're low on mana. Rely on discard to win before they can cast, or use cheap, efficient threats that are hard to counter.
Early disruption is key. Thoughtseize their mana rocks or ramp spells. Answer their early threats efficiently. Don't let them stabilize; pressure them with threats and discard.

It's a game of hand efficiency and graveyard advantage. Prioritize Thoughtseize on their key threats or discard. Use Graveyard Trespasser to disrupt their recursion. Win by having better card selection and managing the graveyard better.

common_threats: Watch for opponent's discard spells (they'll try to empty your hand).
Track their graveyard (Trespasser needs to know what's there).
Note if they're playing sweepers (you need to have threats out or answers ready).
Watch for mana development (if they're ramping, you need to disrupt early).
